verdict_reasoning: BAX is a pro-apoptotic BCL2 family member canonically resident on the mitochondrial outer membrane (MOM) facing the cytosol. However, BAX undergoes documented translocation to the plasma membrane during apoptosis and cell death, where it forms pores and mediates membrane permeabilization. Additionally, BAX has been detected on the cell surface of apoptotic cells and is involved in externalization of phosphatidylserine and other death-pathway markers. This cell-death-induced surface display qualifies as contextual surface accessibility via the cell_state_induced mechanism.
predicted_key_uncertainty: Extent and functional relevance of BAX PM localization during apoptosis versus its dominant mitochondrial role; whether PM-resident BAX constitutes a therapeutically targetable surface pool or is merely a transient late-stage apoptotic event.
